title: What I think about Affirmative Action

transcript.text: Hello, my name is Kyle Pounds, and this is my video, What I Think About Affirmative Action.
Number one, it's very hard to just blatantly talk about affirmative action.
This is the one, this is an issue where you really need to do it in a case-by-case scenario.
And that we should also understand that it's something that's always in constant flux
And should be re-evaluated every single year.
And I never just left to run wild, because it will definitely become unfair,
That it's always moving and changing.
But, that being said, I'll go ahead and read some reasons for affirmative action.
Like it leads to diversity.
Diversity, you can use affirmative action to get different communities more mixed in racially,
And I think we should definitely try and get our communities as mixed racially as we can.
And, yeah, it helps people who are poor.
We definitely want a whole, you know, if we have a big group of poor people,
We should try and bring those people up and help them out.
Obviously, case-by-case scenario.
Yeah, and it compensates minorities for centuries of slavery and oppression.
It's obvious to me that, well, to everybody,
That black people are poorer than white people in America.
And it's the human race.
It's not black race, white race.
And, ideally, in an ideal world, everybody would have the same amount of money,
Have the same status and power.
So, obviously, the black people need to catch up.
You don't have to be a genius to figure that one out.
So, why would you not help them?
Obviously, you would help them.
You, and, you know, the argument against that would be,
Oh, but they're, you know, they can't do the job as well.
That wouldn't be a meritocracy.
Well, I mean, don't let it run out of control.
You obviously want to keep your eye on the ball.
We evaluate them all the time.
But people, people, everybody can do a job.
You don't have to be, like, qualified to do a job.
You know, it's not going to, like, tear a hole in the fabric of society
If you get a couple of people who aren't quite as qualified
Because of their unfortunate circumstances, background.
They'll catch up, you know, and other people can have to pick up the slack for them.
That's not a good, I don't like that argument.
So, yeah.
That's basically what I think about affirmative action.
If anybody ever has a problem with affirmative action,
I would behoove them to give a reason, you know.
Say, like, oh, this community already has so-and-so of this race of people.
We don't need any more.
Something like that, you know.
Not just kind of blankly say.
Just go on a bunch of sound bites and stuff.
That's just stupid.
Okay.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
